In VS Code create a new file `agents/live_monitor.py` and paste this: ```python import sys import os import time import yfinance as yf import gspread from datetime import datetime from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))) from config import TICKERS, SECTORS, STARTING_CAPITAL, NOTIFICATION_EMAIL, GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD from agents.sentiment import get_regime from agents.options_strategy import analyze_trade, STRATEGY_RULES from agents.options_paper import log_trade from ml.predict import predict_win_probability import smtplib from email.mime.text import MIMEText from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart def clean(val): try: if val != val: return 0 return round(float(val), 2) except: return 0 def get_sheets(): scope = ["https://spreadsheets.google.com/feeds", "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive"] creds = Credentials.from_service_account_file("credentials.json", scopes=scope) client = gspread.authorize(creds) return client.open("Trading Bot Log") def get_already_alerted_today(): """Returns a set of tickers already alerted today, using a sheet tab as the tracker.""" try: sheet = get_sheets() try: tracker = sheet.worksheet("Daily Alerts Tracker") except gspread.exceptions.WorksheetNotFound: tracker = sheet.add_worksheet(title="Daily Alerts Tracker", rows=1000, cols=3) tracker.append_row(["Date", "Ticker", "Time"]) return set() rows = tracker.get_all_values() today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d") alerted = set() for row in rows[1:]: if len(row) >= 2 and row[0] == today: alerted.add(row[1]) return alerted except Exception as e: print(f"Error reading alert tracker: {e}") return set() def mark_alerted(ticker): try: sheet = get_sheets() tracker = sheet.worksheet("Daily Alerts Tracker") now = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M") today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d") tracker.append_row([today, ticker, now]) except Exception as e: print(f"Error marking alert: {e}") def quick_score_stock(ticker, sector, etf_return, vix_price): """Lightweight in-memory scoring -- same math as outcomes.py, no sheet writes.""" try: df = yf.Ticker(ticker).history(period="1y") if df.empty or len(df) < 200: return None closes = df["Close"].dropna() delta = closes.diff() gain = delta.where(delta > 0, 0).rolling(14).mean() loss = -delta.where(delta < 0, 0).rolling(14).mean() rs = gain / loss rsi = float(100 - (100 / (1 + rs.iloc[-1]))) ma20 = closes.rolling(20).mean() bb_lower = ma20 - 2 * closes.rolling(20).std() bb_upper = ma20 + 2 * closes.rolling(20).std() ema12 = closes.ewm(span=12).mean() ema26 = closes.ewm(span=26).mean() macd = ema12 - ema26 signal_line = macd.ewm(span=9).mean() ma50 = closes.rolling(50).mean() ma200 = closes.rolling(200).mean() vol_avg = df["Volume"].rolling(20).mean() price = float(closes.iloc[-1]) bb_low = float(bb_lower.iloc[-1]) ma50_val = float(ma50.iloc[-1]) ma200_val = float(ma200.iloc[-1]) macd_val = float(macd.iloc[-1]) macd_sig = float(signal_line.iloc[-1]) vol_now = float(df["Volume"].iloc[-1]) vol_avg_val = float(vol_avg.iloc[-1]) if any(v != v for v in [price, rsi, macd_val, macd_sig, bb_low, ma50_val, ma200_val]): return None volume_spike = vol_now > vol_avg_val lookback = min(20, len(closes) - 1) stock_return = (closes.iloc[-1] / closes.iloc[-lookback] - 1) * 100 rel_strength = float(stock_return) - float(etf_return) if rel_strength != rel_strength: rel_strength = 0 mr_score = 0 if rsi < 35: mr_score += 0.5 if price <= bb_low: mr_score += 0.5 if macd_val > macd_sig: mr_score += 0.5 trend_score = 0 if ma50_val > ma200_val: trend_score += 1.0 if price > ma50_val: trend_score += 1.0 sector_score = 0 if rel_strength > 0.02: sector_score = 1.5 elif rel_strength > 0: sector_score = 0.75 vol_score = 0.5 if volume_spike else 0 if rsi < 40 and price <= bb_low: trade_mode = "MEAN_REVERSION" weighted = (mr_score * 1.5) + (trend_score * 0.5) + sector_score + vol_score elif ma50_val > ma200_val and price > ma50_val: trade_mode = "MOMENTUM" weighted = (mr_score * 0.5) + (trend_score * 1.5) + sector_score + vol_score else: trade_mode = "NEUTRAL" weighted = mr_score + trend_score + sector_score + vol_score score = round(min(weighted, 10), 2) return { "ticker": ticker, "sector": sector, "price": price, "score": score, "trend_score": trend_score, "sector_score": sector_score, "mr_score": mr_score, "vol_score": vol_score, "trade_mode": trade_mode, "rsi": rsi } except Exception: return None def get_news_headlines(ticker, max_items=4): try: stock = yf.Ticker(ticker) news = stock.news[:max_items] if stock.news else [] headlines = [] for item in news: title = item.get("content", {}).get("title", "") link = item.get("content", {}).get("canonicalUrl", {}).get("url", "") if title: headlines.append({"title": title, "url": link}) return headlines except Exception: return [] def send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime): spread = trade["spread"] if trade["strategy"] == "SELL_PUT_SPREAD": spread_desc = f"Sell ${spread['short_strike']}P / Buy ${spread['long_strike']}P" credit_text = f"Credit: ${spread['net_credit']}" iv = spread.get("short_iv", 0) else: spread_desc = f"Buy ${spread['long_strike']}C / Sell ${spread['short_strike']}C" credit_text = f"Debit: ${spread['net_debit']}" iv = spread.get("long_iv", 0) news_html = "" if headlines: for h in headlines: news_html += f'
  • {h["title"]}
    • ' else: news_html = '
  • No recent headlines found
    • ' now = datetime.now().strftime("%B %d, %Y %I:%M %p CDT") html = f"""

    ⚡ Intraday Signal — {trade['ticker']}

    {now} | Regime: {regime}

    Score

    {trade['score']}

    ML Probability

    {ml_prob}%

    Confidence

    {trade['confidence']}

    Trade Setup

    Strategy: {trade['strategy'].replace('_',' ')}

    Spread: {spread_desc}

    {credit_text} | ROR: {spread['return_on_risk']}%

    Breakeven: ${spread['breakeven']} | IV: {iv}% | Exp: {spread['expiration']}

    Contracts: {trade['contracts']} | Total Risk: ${trade['total_risk']} | Size: {trade['position_pct']}% of portfolio

    Recent News

      {news_html}

    Paper trade logged automatically. Trading Bot — Intraday Alert

    """ try: msg = MIMEMultipart("alternative") msg['From'] = NOTIFICATION_EMAIL msg['To'] = NOTIFICATION_EMAIL msg['Subject'] = f"⚡ Trade Signal: {trade['ticker']} | {trade['confidence']} | {ml_prob}% ML prob" msg.attach(MIMEText(html, 'html')) server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587) server.starttls() server.login(NOTIFICATION_EMAIL, GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD) server.send_message(msg) server.quit() print(f"Alert email sent for {trade['ticker']}") except Exception as e: print(f"Error sending alert email: {e}") def run_live_monitor(): print(f"\n{'='*50}") print(f"LIVE MONITOR — {datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M')}") print(f"{'='*50}") print("Checking market regime...") regime_data = get_regime() regime = regime_data["regime"] vix_price = regime_data.get("vix", 20.0) already_alerted = get_already_alerted_today() print(f"Already alerted today: {already_alerted if already_alerted else 'none'}") print("Fetching sector ETF returns...") etf_returns = {} for sector, etf in SECTORS.items(): try: df = yf.Ticker(etf).history(period="1mo") closes = df["Close"].dropna() if len(closes) > 20: ret = (closes.iloc[-1] / closes.iloc[-20] - 1) * 100 etf_returns[sector] = float(ret) if ret == ret else 0 else: etf_returns[sector] = 0 except: etf_returns[sector] = 0 new_alerts = 0 checked = 0 for sector, tickers in TICKERS.items(): etf_ret = etf_returns.get(sector, 0) for ticker in tickers: if ticker in already_alerted: continue checked += 1 sig = quick_score_stock(ticker, sector, etf_ret, vix_price) if sig is None: continue key = (sig["trade_mode"], regime) rule = STRATEGY_RULES.get(key) if not rule or rule["strategy"] == "NO_TRADE": continue if sig["score"] < 3.5: continue try: ml_prob = predict_win_probability( score=sig["score"], trend_score=sig["trend_score"], sector_score=sig["sector_score"], mr_score=sig["mr_score"], vol_score=sig["vol_score"], vix=vix_price, sector=sector, mode=sig["trade_mode"], regime=regime ) except Exception: ml_prob = 50.0 if ml_prob < 55: continue print(f"\n>>> SIGNAL FOUND: {ticker} | Score {sig['score']} | ML {ml_prob}% | {sig['trade_mode']} + {regime}") trade = analyze_trade(ticker, sector, sig["score"], sig["trade_mode"], regime, sig["price"], STARTING_CAPITAL) if trade is None: print(f" Could not build options trade for {ticker}, skipping alert") continue log_trade(trade) headlines = get_news_headlines(ticker) send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime) mark_alerted(ticker) new_alerts += 1 time.sleep(1.5) print(f"\n{'='*50}") print(f"LIVE MONITOR COMPLETE") print(f"Stocks checked: {checked}") print(f"New alerts sent: {new_alerts}") print(f"{'='*50}") if __name__ == "__main__": run_live_monitor() ``` A few notes on what this does and the decisions baked in: - Uses your **already-updated `STRATEGY_RULES`** (the corrected, deduplicated version) to decide if a mode+regime combo is even worth checking - Adds an **ML probability floor of 55%** as a second gate on top of the rule-based check — so it needs both the rule system AND the model to agree before alerting - Writes to a new **"Daily Alerts Tracker"** tab (auto-created on first run) to prevent duplicate alerts for the same ticker on the same day - Pulls free yfinance headlines, no Claude API cost yet - Logs the paper trade exactly like your main pipeline does, so it shows up in your existing Options Paper Trades tracking and portfolio summary Save it, then verify and test: ``` python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/live_monitor.py'); print('No syntax errors!')" python3 agents/live_monitor.py ``` This will scan all 377 stocks once (taking a while, similar to outcomes.py) and only email you if something actually clears both bars. Let me know what it prints — and once it works, we'll add the GitHub Actions schedule to run it every 30 minutes during market hours.

Let's tighten this before it goes on a 30-minute schedule, or you'll get flooded with emails by lunchtime.

```

import gspread

Now we can see exactly what happened, and it confirms the real problem — this is **not** 22 genuinely diverse, high-conviction signals. This is **one single event**: an entire sector (Semiconductors) moving together in lockstep, triggering 19 near-identical alerts for the same underlying cause. That's the sector correlation problem your `risk.py` was specifically built to catch in the main pipeline — and `live_monitor.py` is bypassing it entirely.

Three real problems to fix, in order of severity:

**1. All 19 of these are "LOW" confidence — they should never have alerted at all.**

Look back at your corrected `STRATEGY_RULES`:
```python
("MOMENTUM", "NEUTRAL"): {"strategy": "SELL_PUT_SPREAD", "confidence": "LOW", "size_pct": 0.02},
```

LOW confidence means we explicitly found this combination barely beats a coin flip (53.9% WR on the deduplicated data). The live monitor's gate was "score ≥ 3.5 AND ML probability ≥ 55%" — but it never checked confidence tier at all. That's the bug. We need to hard-block LOW and AVOID confidence from ever triggering an intraday alert; only HIGH (and maybe MEDIUM with a higher score bar) should page you.

**2. No sector/correlation deduplication.** 19 semiconductor names firing within the same scan is the entire sector moving, not 19 independent opportunities. If SOXX is up sharply today, nearly every chip stock will show MOMENTUM. We need a rule like "max 1-2 alerts per sector per day" mirroring your existing `MAX_SECTOR_POSITIONS` limit from `risk.py`.

**3. The Daily Alerts Tracker is working correctly** (no duplicate tickers, all logged with timestamps) — so the dedupe-by-ticker piece is fine, it's the missing confidence/sector gates that caused the flood.

Let's fix both in `agents/live_monitor.py`. In VS Code find this section inside `run_live_monitor()`:

```python
            key = (sig["trade_mode"], regime)
            rule = STRATEGY_RULES.get(key)
            if not rule or rule["strategy"] == "NO_TRADE":
                continue
            if sig["score"] < 3.5:
                continue
```

Replace with:

```python
            key = (sig["trade_mode"], regime)
            rule = STRATEGY_RULES.get(key)
            if not rule or rule["strategy"] == "NO_TRADE":
                continue
            if rule["confidence"] not in ("HIGH", "MEDIUM"):
                continue
            if rule["confidence"] == "HIGH" and sig["score"] < 4.0:
                continue
            if rule["confidence"] == "MEDIUM" and sig["score"] < 4.5:
                continue
```

Then add sector-limiting. Right before the main `for sector, tickers in TICKERS.items():` loop, add:

```python
    sector_alert_count = {}
    MAX_ALERTS_PER_SECTOR = 1
```

And inside the loop, right after the confidence checks above, add:

```python
            if sector_alert_count.get(sector, 0) >= MAX_ALERTS_PER_SECTOR:
                continue
```

And right after a successful alert is sent (after `mark_alerted(ticker)`), add:

```python
            sector_alert_count[sector] = sector_alert_count.get(sector, 0) + 1
```

Save with **Command + S**, verify, and rerun:

```
python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/live_monitor.py'); print('No syntax errors!')"
python3 agents/live_monitor.py
```

This time it should send close to 0-3 alerts total — only HIGH confidence (which right now is essentially MOMENTUM_BULL only, per the deduplicated data) plus at most 1 per sector. That's the volume you actually want for a "this is genuinely worth interrupting your day for" email. Run it and tell me what it prints.

import gspread

    tracker = sheet.worksheet('Survivorship Removed')
except gspread.exceptions.WorksheetNotFound:
    tracker = sheet.add_worksheet(title='Survivorship Removed', rows=200, cols=4)
    tracker.append_row(['Date Removed', 'Sector', 'Ticker', 'Reason'])

dead_tickers = [
    ('Infrastructure', 'SJW'),
    ('Finance', 'CMA'),
    ('Finance', 'DFS'),
    ('Health', 'HOLX'),
    ('Backdoor Tech', 'PSTG'),
    ('Consumer Discretionary', 'HBI'),
    ('Materials', 'VNTR'),
    ('Materials', 'GCP'),
    ('Materials', 'CTRA'),
]

today = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
for sector, ticker in dead_tickers:
    tracker.append_row([today, sector, ticker, 'No price data / possibly delisted, acquired, or renamed'])

print(f'Logged {len(dead_tickers)} removed tickers to Survivorship Removed tab')

9 dead tickers out of 377 — about 2.4% of your universe, consistent with what you'd expect over a year of M&A activity. Let's handle this properly per the survivorship bias principle: log them before removing them, rather than just deleting silently.

First, let's create the survivorship tracker and log these:

```
python3 -c "
import gspread
from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials
from datetime import datetime

scope = ['https://spreadsheets.google.com/feeds', 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive']
creds = Credentials.from_service_account_file('credentials.json', scopes=scope)
client = gspread.authorize(creds)
sheet = client.open('Trading Bot Log')

try:
    tracker = sheet.worksheet('Survivorship Removed')
except gspread.exceptions.WorksheetNotFound:
    tracker = sheet.add_worksheet(title='Survivorship Removed', rows=200, cols=4)
    tracker.append_row(['Date Removed', 'Sector', 'Ticker', 'Reason'])

dead_tickers = [
    ('Infrastructure', 'SJW'),
    ('Finance', 'CMA'),
    ('Finance', 'DFS'),
    ('Health', 'HOLX'),
    ('Backdoor Tech', 'PSTG'),
    ('Consumer Discretionary', 'HBI'),
    ('Materials', 'VNTR'),
    ('Materials', 'GCP'),
    ('Materials', 'CTRA'),
]

today = datetime.now().strftime('%Y-%m-%d')
for sector, ticker in dead_tickers:
    tracker.append_row([today, sector, ticker, 'No price data / possibly delisted, acquired, or renamed'])

print(f'Logged {len(dead_tickers)} removed tickers to Survivorship Removed tab')
"
```

Now let's remove them from `config.py`. In VS Code open `config.py` and find each of these lines in the `TICKERS` dictionary, then delete just that ticker (and its surrounding comma) from each list:

- **Infrastructure**: remove `"SJW",`
- **Finance**: remove `"CMA",` and `"DFS",`
- **Health**: remove `"HOLX",`
- **Backdoor Tech**: remove `"PSTG",`
- **Consumer Discretionary**: remove `"HBI",`
- **Materials**: remove `"VNTR",`, `"GCP",`, and `"CTRA"` (careful with this last one — check if it's followed by a comma or is the last item in the list, adjust accordingly so you don't leave a trailing comma issue or double comma)

Once you've made those edits, save with **Command + S** and verify:

```
python3 -c "
from config import TICKERS
total = sum(len(v) for v in TICKERS.values())
print(f'Total tickers now: {total}')
for sector in TICKERS:

"

368 tickers, exactly as expected — clean removal across all 9 sectors. Let's push this and the live_monitor fix to GitHub:

```
git add .
git commit -m "Remove 9 delisted tickers, log to Survivorship Removed tab, fix live_monitor confidence/sector gates"
git push
```

**Now, two important loose ends before we schedule this on GitHub Actions:**

**1. Backtest data integrity** — your existing 4-window backtest (~18,400 rows) and the Random Forest model trained on it still contain data from these 9 dead tickers. That's not necessarily wrong (they were real, tradeable stocks during those historical windows), but worth knowing: if any of them got acquired mid-window, their final price data near the acquisition date could be distorted (M&A often causes a stock to jump or freeze near the deal price). Not worth re-running the whole backtest over 9 tickers out of 368, but flag it as a known minor data quality note rather than something to fix right now.

**2. The daily tracker reset** — this is the one we still need to build before scheduling 30-min runs. Right now "Daily Alerts Tracker" just accumulates forever; nothing clears it at the start of a new day. Let's fix that now.

In VS Code open `agents/live_monitor.py` and find `get_already_alerted_today()`. Right after the `rows = tracker.get_all_values()` line, add a cleanup step that prunes anything older than today (keeping the sheet small and making "already alerted" checks always accurate without needing a separate reset job):

Find:
```python
        rows = tracker.get_all_values()
        today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        alerted = set()
        for row in rows[1:]:
            if len(row) >= 2 and row[0] == today:
                alerted.add(row[1])
        return alerted
```

Replace with:
```python
        rows = tracker.get_all_values()
        today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d")
        alerted = set()
        keep_rows = [rows[0]] if rows else [["Date", "Ticker", "Time"]]

        for row in rows[1:]:
            if len(row) >= 2 and row[0] == today:
                alerted.add(row[1])
                keep_rows.append(row)
            # rows from previous days are simply dropped -- no longer relevant
            # for "already alerted today" and keeps the tab from growing forever

        if len(keep_rows) < len(rows):
            tracker.clear()
            tracker.update(values=keep_rows, range_name='A1')

        return alerted
```

This means every time `live_monitor.py` runs, it automatically prunes anything not from today before checking — so the first run each morning naturally clears yesterday's alerts without needing a separate scheduled reset job.

Save with **Command + S**, verify, and push:

```
python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/live_monitor.py'); print('No syntax errors!')"
git add .
git commit -m "Auto-prune alert tracker to current day only"
git push
```

Now let's add the GitHub Actions schedule. In VS Code open `.github/workflows/scanner.yml` and add a second job (separate from your existing `scan` job) so the 30-min live monitor runs independently of your 3x/day full scan:

Find the end of your existing `jobs:` section and add this as a sibling job (same indentation level as `scan:`):

```yaml
  live_monitor:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v5
        with:
          python-version: "3.11"

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: pip install yfinance pandas gspread google-auth anthropic scikit-learn numpy

      - name: Create credentials file
        env:
          GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS: ${{ secrets.GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS }}
        run: echo "$GOOGLE_CREDENTIALS" > credentials.json

      - name: Run live monitor
        env:
          GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD: ${{ secrets.GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD }}
          NOTIFICATION_EMAIL: ${{ secrets.NOTIFICATION_EMAIL }}
        run: python3 agents/live_monitor.py
```

And update the `on: schedule:` section at the very top to add the 30-minute cron pattern. Find:

```yaml
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 14 * * 1-5'
    - cron: '0 17 * * 1-5'
    - cron: '30 21 * * 1-5'
  workflow_dispatch:
```

Replace with (adding 30-min slots from 9:30am to 4pm CDT, which is 14:30-21:00 UTC):

```yaml
on:
  schedule:
    - cron: '0 14 * * 1-5'
    - cron: '0 17 * * 1-5'
    - cron: '30 21 * * 1-5'
    - cron: '30,0 14-21 * * 1-5'
  workflow_dispatch:
```

**Important catch though** — right now both `scan` and `live_monitor` jobs would trigger on every single cron entry in that shared schedule list, since GitHub Actions doesn't let one job listen to only some crons in the same `schedule:` block without extra logic. We need to split them into conditional checks based on the time. Rather than fight that YAML limitation, the cleaner fix is **two separate workflow files** — keep your existing `scanner.yml` exactly as it is with its 3 daily crons calling the `scan` job, and create a brand new file `.github/workflows/live_monitor.yml` with its own independent 30-min schedule calling only `live_monitor.py`.

Want me to write that second workflow file now as its own clean file instead of trying to merge it into the existing one? That avoids the cron-job-mixing problem entirely and is more in line with how your existing two systems (outcomes.py vs main.py) are already cleanly separated.

chat_messages.text:
This run history is the real smoking gun, and it's important you see it clearly because it explains everything — both the $11.21 charge and why it's about to happen again unless we act now.

**Look at the durations:**

- `Trading Bot Scanner #57: 1h 36m 17s`
- `Trading Bot Scanner #56: 2h 48m 10s` 😬
- `Trading Bot Scanner #59, #58: ~28-29 min` (these are more normal)
- `Live Monitor` runs: mostly 1-2 minutes (these are cheap and fine)

**`Trading Bot Scanner` is supposed to take about 25-30 minutes** (368 stocks at ~1.75-2.5 sec each, plus options/sentiment steps). A 2-hour-48-minute run and a 1-hour-36-minute run are **not normal** — something is hanging or retrying excessively in `outcomes.py` or `main.py`, almost certainly a yfinance rate-limit/timeout loop with no upper bound, or a Google Sheets API retry storm. That single 2h48m run alone likely ate more minutes than `Live Monitor` did all week.

This is the real cost driver, not `live_monitor.py`. **`Live Monitor` is actually cheap and well-behaved** — 1-2 minutes per run × 14/day ≈ 14-28 min/day total, which is fine. The 3x/day `scanner.yml` job occasionally exploding to 1.5-3 hours is what blew through 2,000 minutes.

**Immediate fix — add a hard timeout so a hung run can never burn unlimited minutes again.** In VS Code open `.github/workflows/scanner.yml` and find the `scan:` job. Add a `timeout-minutes` line right under `runs-on:`:

Find:
```yaml
jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
```

Replace with:
```yaml
jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 40
    steps:
```

This caps any single `outcomes.py`/`main.py` run at 40 minutes max — if it hangs, GitHub kills it automatically instead of letting it run for 3 hours. Do the same in `live_monitor.yml` as a safety net, even though it's behaving fine currently:

```yaml
jobs:
  monitor:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
```

Save both, then push:

```
git add .
git commit -m "Add hard timeouts to prevent runaway Actions minutes usage"
git push
```

**Given you've now had a real $11.21 charge from this exact failure mode, I'd strongly recommend making the repo public anyway as the permanent backstop** — even with timeouts in place, a few accidental 40-minute-max overruns per week across 17 total daily runs (3 scanner + 14 monitor) will still add up over a month on a private repo's 2,000-minute cap. Public repos remove the cap entirely so a misbehaving run costs you nothing instead of money.

Want to do the public repo switch now (Settings → Danger Zone → Change visibility), on top of the timeout fix? That combination — hard timeout + unlimited public minutes — means this can never cost you money again, full stop.

chat_messages.text:
Found it — there's a typo in the filename: `live_,monitor.yml` has a stray comma in it instead of `live_monitor.yml`. That's almost certainly from when the file was created in VS Code and a comma got typed by accident instead of (or along with) the underscore. GitHub Actions doesn't care about filenames being "correct" — it just runs whatever `.yml` files exist in that folder, comma and all, which is why it's been working despite the typo.

Let's rename it properly:

```
mv "./.github/workflows/live_,monitor.yml" "./.github/workflows/live_monitor.yml"
```

Then verify:
```
find . -iname "*.yml"
```

You should now see `./.github/workflows/live_monitor.yml` with no comma. Now let's add the timeout line. In VS Code, open this corrected `live_monitor.yml` file and find:

```yaml
jobs:
  monitor:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
```

Replace with:
```yaml
jobs:
  monitor:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    timeout-minutes: 10
    steps:
```

Save with **Command + S**, then push the rename + the timeout fix together:

```
git add .
git commit -m "Fix workflow filename typo, add timeout to live_monitor"
git push
```

One thing to double check after this push — go to GitHub Actions in your browser and confirm a workflow still shows up called **"Live Monitor"** (not a broken/duplicate one), since renaming the file could theoretically make GitHub treat it as a brand new workflow rather than a continuation of the old one. If it shows up fine, we're done here — then let's go check those live paper trade numbers.

chat_messages.text:
This number needs to be treated with real suspicion, not celebration — a 100% win rate across 21 closed trades and +161.95% return in what's only been a few days of live operation is not plausible for a strategy whose own deduplicated backtest showed a 57-65% win rate at best. Something here is producing fake or badly-miscalculated results, not genuine performance.

Let's find the actual cause rather than assume the system is just "doing amazing." A few likely culprits, in order of probability:

**1. The exit-check math in `agents/options_paper.py` is broken.** Earlier today (in this same conversation) we saw it close CRWD and AAPL trades for big wins very quickly — that's the same `check_open_trades()` function. If the spread valuation logic that estimates "current spread value" is systematically biased toward showing profit (e.g. always assuming the stock stayed safely above the short strike), every single trade will register as a win regardless of what actually happened, which would exactly produce a 100% win rate.

**2. Trades are closing almost immediately rather than running their full hold period**, hitting the 50%-profit-target branch on day one or two because of a pricing bug, not real market movement.

**3. Position sizing/contracts math could be inflating P&L per trade** — if `contracts` calculation is off, a real $50-100 win could be getting reported as a $1,000+ win.

Let's pull the actual closed trade log and look at real numbers, not just the summary:

```
python3 -c "
import gspread
from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials

scope = ['https://spreadsheets.google.com/feeds', 'https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive']
creds = Credentials.from_service_account_file('credentials.json', scopes=scope)
client = gspread.authorize(creds)
sheet = client.open('Trading Bot Log')
opt = sheet.worksheet('Options Paper Trades')
rows = opt.get_all_values()

print('Header:', rows[0])
print()
print('Closed trades detail:')
for row in rows[1:]:
    if len(row) >= 24 and row[19] == 'CLOSED':
        print(f'{row[2]:<6} Entry: {row[1]} | Exit: {row[20]} | Credit/Debit: {row[12]} | Max Loss: {row[13]} | PnL: {row[22]} | PnL%: {row[23]}')
"
```

This will show every closed trade's actual entry/exit dates and reported PnL side by side. I'd bet money (figuratively) that you'll see trades closing the very next day or two after opening, every single time, with suspiciously large PnL relative to the credit/max-loss numbers logged at entry — that's the fingerprint of the valuation bug, not real edge. Run it and paste the output, and we'll trace the exact bad math in `check_open_trades()`.

chat_messages.text:
This confirms exactly what I suspected, and it's good that we caught it before any real money was involved. This is not real performance — this is a **valuation bug producing fake, impossible wins**, and here's the precise evidence:

**Every single one of these 21 trades is the exact same 19 semiconductor positions** that fired in that flooded batch on June 24th (the same group we identified as a sector-correlation bug earlier — INTC, QCOM, MU, AMAT, LRCX, KLAC, ASML, ARM, MRVL, SWKS, QRVO, SITM, ALGM, ACLS, ONTO, UCTT, COHU, ICHR, PDFS, SLAB, UMC). They all opened within the same 3-minute window, and **all 21 closed roughly 36 hours later, every single one registering as a profitable win.**

**That's mathematically impossible for genuine bull-put-spread P&L.** A real bull put spread only profits if the underlying stock stays above the short strike — it cannot be true that 21 different semiconductor stocks, opened simultaneously, all independently stayed above their respective short strikes within 36 hours with zero exceptions. Real markets don't work that way even in a strong rally.

**The actual bug is in `check_open_trades()`'s spread valuation logic.** Look back at the code we wrote earlier:

```python
if current_price > short_strike:
    current_spread_value = 0.05
elif current_price < long_strike:
    current_spread_value = short_strike - long_strike
else:
    pct_through = (short_strike - current_price) / (short_strike - long_strike)
    current_spread_value = round((short_strike - long_strike) * pct_through * 0.8, 2)

current_pnl = round((entry_credit - current_spread_value) * contracts * 100, 2)
max_profit = round(entry_credit * contracts * 100, 2)
```

If `current_price > short_strike` for literally all 21 names (entirely plausible if the whole sector rallied together, which is exactly the correlation problem we already knew about), then `current_spread_value = 0.05` for every single one, which makes `current_pnl ≈ entry_credit × contracts × 100` — basically the **maximum possible profit, every time**, regardless of how close the actual price is to the strike or how much time has actually passed. There's no partial credit, no realistic decay curve, nothing that could ever produce a loss in this branch. That's the bug: **the moment price clears the short strike at all, it's coded as instant max profit**, with zero connection to actual options pricing reality.

**Combined with the sector-correlation bug** (19 nearly-identical semiconductor trades from one signal event, not 21 independent opportunities), this isn't "21 trades winning" — it's **one single event, with one shared (real or coincidental) sector rally, run through a fake-profit valuation formula 21 times.**

**The fix — replace the fake valuation with the actual options pricing model**, using the real current options chain (the same thing `options_strategy.py` already pulls when opening a trade) instead of a hand-waved linear approximation. In VS Code open `agents/options_paper.py` and find the `check_open_trades()` function's pricing section. Replace the entire estimation block:

Find:

                    long_row = puts[puts['strike'] == long_strike]

                    if short_row.empty or long_row.empty:
                        print(f"  Could not find current quotes for {ticker} strikes, skipping")
                        continue

                    short_now = round((short_row.iloc[0]['bid'] + short_row.iloc[0]['ask']) / 2, 2)
                    long_now = round((long_row.iloc[0]['bid'] + long_row.iloc[0]['ask']) / 2, 2)
                    current_spread_value = round(short_now - long_now, 2)

                    current_pnl = round((entry_credit - current_spread_value) * contracts * 100, 2)
                    max_profit = round(entry_credit * contracts * 100, 2)

                else:
                    long_strike = float(spread_desc.split('$')[1].split('C')[0])
                    short_strike = float(spread_desc.split('$')[2].split('C')[0])
                    entry_debit = abs(entry_credit)
                    calls = chain.calls

                    long_row = calls[calls['strike'] == long_strike]
                    short_row = calls[calls['strike'] == short_strike]

                    if long_row.empty or short_row.empty:
                        print(f"  Could not find current quotes for {ticker} strikes, skipping")
                        continue

                    long_now = round((long_row.iloc[0]['bid'] + long_row.iloc[0]['ask']) / 2, 2)
                    short_now = round((short_row.iloc[0]['bid'] + short_row.iloc[0]['ask']) / 2, 2)
                    current_spread_value = round(long_now - short_now, 2)

                    current_pnl = round((current_spread_value - entry_debit) * contracts * 100, 2)
                    max_profit = round((short_strike - long_strike - entry_debit) * contracts * 100, 2)
```

This uses real, live bid/ask prices for the exact strikes you're holding — the same data source `options_strategy.py` already trusts when opening a trade — so the P&L reflects actual market-implied value, not a guessed linear ramp that breaks the moment price crosses the strike.

                    short_now = round((short_row.iloc[0]['bid'] + short_row.iloc[0]['ask']) / 2, 2)
                    current_spread_value = round(long_now - short_now, 2)

                    current_pnl = round((current_spread_value - entry_debit) * contracts * 100, 2)
                    max_profit = round((short_strike - long_strike - entry_debit) * contracts * 100, 2)
```

This uses real, live bid/ask prices for the exact strikes you're holding — the same data source `options_strategy.py` already trusts when opening a trade — so the P&L reflects actual market-implied value, not a guessed linear ramp that breaks the moment price crosses the strike.

You're right to push back on that — "1 per sector" was a blunt instrument to stop the flood, but it throws away real information. If an entire sector is genuinely moving together with strong, independent signals, that's actually useful to know and potentially worth acting on more than once. The smarter fix isn't "limit how many you can catch," it's "size and treat correlated positions as what they actually are — one shared bet — rather than pretending each one is independent risk."

Here's a better design:

**The real problem isn't alert volume — it's that the system was treating 19 correlated positions as if they were 19 independent ones for risk-sizing purposes.** Your own `agents/risk.py` already solved this exact problem for the main pipeline months ago — it checks pairwise correlation between approved tickers and caps exposure per sector. `live_monitor.py` just never got that same protection. Let's port it over properly instead of using a blunt 1-per-sector cap.

**The smarter system:**

1. **Allow multiple alerts per sector**, but compute real pairwise correlation between any new candidate and tickers already alerted today (same approach `risk.py` already uses with `yf.download` + `.corr()`)
2. **If a new signal is highly correlated (>0.7, same threshold as `risk.py`) with something already alerted today**, still send the alert — but explicitly flag it in the email as "correlated with [ticker], same underlying move" and reduce its position size automatically (e.g. half-size if 2nd in a correlated cluster, smaller still for 3rd+) so the *combined* exposure to that sector-wide move stays sane, instead of just refusing to tell you about it.
3. **Track cumulative sector exposure in dollars, not alert count.** Cap it at something like `MAX_POSITION_PCT × 3` (today, 15% of $10k = $1,500) total across any single sector per day, regardless of how many individual tickers are involved — once that cap is hit, further same-sector signals get logged and emailed as "informational only, sector exposure cap reached" rather than opening a new paper position.

This way: a real, broad, independently-confirmed sector rally still gets fully surfaced to you (you see all 19 names, not just 1), but the actual capital and risk exposure is sized like the single correlated bet it really is, not 19x overstated risk.

Want me to build this into `agents/live_monitor.py` now — reusing the correlation-checking code that already exists in `agents/risk.py` rather than writing it twice?

Let's be deliberate about what "smarter and more adaptive" actually means here, because it can mean several different things, and they're not all the same project:

**1. Smarter risk-awareness (what we were just discussing)** — correctly treating correlated positions as correlated, sizing exposure by real dollar risk per sector/theme rather than per-ticker counts. This is a relatively contained fix, reusing code you already have in `risk.py`.

**2. Smarter signal detection** — catching things the current rule-based system misses entirely. This is where the Random Forest model comes back in: right now it only nudges scores slightly (the ±1.5 adjustment), but it could become the primary detector for entirely new patterns the hand-built rules never anticipated — e.g., "this specific combination of moderate trend + high volume + low RSI in Materials during a NEUTRAL regime has a real edge nobody coded explicitly."

**3. Smarter regime adaptation** — your regime detector currently outputs one of three buckets (BULL/NEUTRAL/BEAR) computed once. A more adaptive system would let regime classification update intraday (you've already got `live_monitor.py` checking it every 30 min) and let the *strategy itself* shift its risk tolerance and position sizing dynamically as regime conditions change mid-day, not just at the 3 daily checkpoints.

**4. Smarter self-correction** — building automated sanity checks so the system catches its own bugs the way you just did, rather than relying on you noticing a suspicious 100% win rate. E.g., a nightly job that flags "any closed trade with PnL% above 50% closed within 48 hours of opening" for manual review, before it can silently accumulate into a misleading track record again.

**My honest recommendation on sequencing:** don't try to overhaul everything simultaneously — that's how systems like this get fragile and hard to debug (you've felt this already today with file corruption issues, indentation bugs, etc. from doing too much in one pass). Instead, tackle these in order of *how much they protect you from being fooled by your own system*, since that's the lesson today just taught you directly:

**Step 2:** Build a small automated "sanity checker" script that runs after every `check_open_trades()` call and flags anything statistically suspicious (instant wins, 100% win streaks, PnL inconsistent with credit/max-loss math) — this is cheap to build and directly prevents today's exact failure mode from happening silently again.

**Step 3:** Then, with those guardrails in place, move on to the bigger "smarter detection" work — letting the ML model take a larger role, possibly retraining it now that you have a cleaner backtest and a corrected options engine.

In VS Code open `agents/live_monitor.py` and let's add a correlation check that runs right before sending an alert, so the position size reflects real shared risk with anything already alerted today. First, add the import at the top. Find: ```python from agents.options_strategy import analyze_trade, STRATEGY_RULES from agents.options_paper import log_trade from ml.predict import predict_win_probability ``` Add right after: ```python from agents.risk import check_correlation ``` Now let's add a correlation-aware sizing function. Add this near the top of the file, after the constants: ```python MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE = STARTING_CAPITAL * 0.15 # cap total $ risk per sector per day CORRELATION_THRESHOLD = 0.7 ``` Then add this new function right after `mark_alerted()`: ```python def get_todays_alerted_tickers_with_sector(target_sector): """Returns tickers already alerted today in the SAME sector, for correlation checking.""" try: sheet = get_sheets() tracker = sheet.worksheet("Daily Alerts Tracker") rows = tracker.get_all_values() today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d") # We need sector info, which isn't stored in the tracker -- pull it # from Options Paper Trades instead, matching today's date + ticker opt = sheet.worksheet("Options Paper Trades") opt_rows = opt.get_all_values() same_sector_today = [] for row in opt_rows[1:]: if len(row) >= 4 and row[1].startswith(today) and row[3] == target_sector: same_sector_today.append(row[2]) # ticker column return same_sector_today except Exception as e: print(f"Error checking sector exposure: {e}") return [] def get_sector_dollar_exposure(sector_tickers): """Sum up total_risk already committed today for a list of tickers, by reading their logged options trades for today.""" try: sheet = get_sheets() opt = sheet.worksheet("Options Paper Trades") rows = opt.get_all_values() today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d") total = 0.0 for row in rows[1:]: if len(row) >= 15 and row[1].startswith(today) and row[2] in sector_tickers: try: total += float(row[14]) # total_risk column except: continue return total except Exception as e: print(f"Error summing sector exposure: {e}") return 0.0 def adjust_size_for_correlation(trade, sector_tickers_today): """ If this new ticker is highly correlated with anything already alerted today in the same sector, scale down its position size to reflect that it's largely the SAME bet, not an independent one. Also enforces a hard cap on total dollar risk per sector per day. """ if not sector_tickers_today: return trade, None # first in this sector today, no adjustment needed try: all_tickers = sector_tickers_today + [trade["ticker"]] corr_pairs = check_correlation(all_tickers) is_correlated = any( trade["ticker"] in (t1, t2) and val > CORRELATION_THRESHOLD for t1, t2, val in corr_pairs ) except Exception: is_correlated = False note = None if is_correlated: # Scale size down based on how many correlated positions already exist today cluster_size = len(sector_tickers_today) + 1 scale_factor = 1 / cluster_size # 2nd in cluster = half size, 3rd = third size, etc. scale_factor = max(scale_factor, 0.25) # never go below 25% of normal size original_risk = trade["total_risk"] trade["contracts"] = max(1, int(trade["contracts"] * scale_factor)) # Recompute risk/credit based on new contract count per_contract_risk = original_risk / max(trade["contracts"] / scale_factor, 1) trade["total_risk"] = round(per_contract_risk * trade["contracts"], 2) trade["position_pct"] = round(trade["position_pct"] * scale_factor, 1) note = (f"Correlated with {', '.join(sector_tickers_today)} (same sector move) -- " f"position size reduced to {round(scale_factor*100)}% of normal") return trade, note ``` Now wire this into the main alert loop. Find: ```python from agents.options_strategy import analyze_trade, STRATEGY_RULES from agents.options_paper import log_trade from ml.predict import predict_win_probability ``` Add right after: ```python from agents.risk import check_correlation ``` Now let's add a correlation-aware sizing function. Add this near the top of the file, after the constants: ```python MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE = STARTING_CAPITAL * 0.15 # cap total $ risk per sector per day CORRELATION_THRESHOLD = 0.7 ``` Then add this new function right after `mark_alerted()`: ```python def get_todays_alerted_tickers_with_sector(target_sector): """Returns tickers already alerted today in the SAME sector, for correlation checking.""" try: sheet = get_sheets() tracker = sheet.worksheet("Daily Alerts Tracker") rows = tracker.get_all_values() today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d") # We need sector info, which isn't stored in the tracker -- pull it # from Options Paper Trades instead, matching today's date + ticker opt = sheet.worksheet("Options Paper Trades") opt_rows = opt.get_all_values() same_sector_today = [] for row in opt_rows[1:]: if len(row) >= 4 and row[1].startswith(today) and row[3] == target_sector: same_sector_today.append(row[2]) # ticker column return same_sector_today except Exception as e: print(f"Error checking sector exposure: {e}") return [] def get_sector_dollar_exposure(sector_tickers): """Sum up total_risk already committed today for a list of tickers, by reading their logged options trades for today.""" try: sheet = get_sheets() opt = sheet.worksheet("Options Paper Trades") rows = opt.get_all_values() today = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d") total = 0.0 for row in rows[1:]: if len(row) >= 15 and row[1].startswith(today) and row[2] in sector_tickers: try: total += float(row[14]) # total_risk column except: continue return total except Exception as e: print(f"Error summing sector exposure: {e}") return 0.0 def adjust_size_for_correlation(trade, sector_tickers_today): """ If this new ticker is highly correlated with anything already alerted today in the same sector, scale down its position size to reflect that it's largely the SAME bet, not an independent one. Also enforces a hard cap on total dollar risk per sector per day. """ if not sector_tickers_today: return trade, None # first in this sector today, no adjustment needed try: all_tickers = sector_tickers_today + [trade["ticker"]] corr_pairs = check_correlation(all_tickers) is_correlated = any( trade["ticker"] in (t1, t2) and val > CORRELATION_THRESHOLD for t1, t2, val in corr_pairs ) except Exception: is_correlated = False note = None if is_correlated: # Scale size down based on how many correlated positions already exist today cluster_size = len(sector_tickers_today) + 1 scale_factor = 1 / cluster_size # 2nd in cluster = half size, 3rd = third size, etc. scale_factor = max(scale_factor, 0.25) # never go below 25% of normal size original_risk = trade["total_risk"] trade["contracts"] = max(1, int(trade["contracts"] * scale_factor)) # Recompute risk/credit based on new contract count per_contract_risk = original_risk / max(trade["contracts"] / scale_factor, 1) trade["total_risk"] = round(per_contract_risk * trade["contracts"], 2) trade["position_pct"] = round(trade["position_pct"] * scale_factor, 1) note = (f"Correlated with {', '.join(sector_tickers_today)} (same sector move) -- " f"position size reduced to {round(scale_factor*100)}% of normal") return trade, note ``` Now wire this into the main alert loop. Find this section inside `run_live_monitor()`: ```python print(f"\n>>> SIGNAL FOUND: {ticker} | Score {sig['score']} | ML {ml_prob}% | {sig['trade_mode']} + {regime} | {rule['confidence']}") trade = analyze_trade(ticker, sector, sig["score"], sig["trade_mode"], regime, sig["price"], STARTING_CAPITAL) if trade is None: print(f" Could not build options trade for {ticker}, skipping alert") continue log_trade(trade) headlines = get_news_headlines(ticker) send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime) mark_alerted(ticker) sector_alert_count[sector] = sector_alert_count.get(sector, 0) + 1 new_alerts += 1 ``` Replace with: ```python print(f"\n>>> SIGNAL FOUND: {ticker} | Score {sig['score']} | ML {ml_prob}% | {sig['trade_mode']} + {regime} | {rule['confidence']}") trade = analyze_trade(ticker, sector, sig["score"], sig["trade_mode"], regime, sig["price"], STARTING_CAPITAL) if trade is None: print(f" Could not build options trade for {ticker}, skipping alert") continue # Check existing sector exposure today -- hard dollar cap regardless # of correlation, prevents one sector from dominating the whole day sector_today_tickers = get_todays_alerted_tickers_with_sector(sector) current_exposure = get_sector_dollar_exposure(sector_today_tickers) if current_exposure >= MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE: print(f" SKIP — {sector} already at ${current_exposure:.0f} exposure today (cap: ${MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE:.0f})") continue # Scale down size if correlated with something already alerted today trade, correlation_note = adjust_size_for_correlation(trade, sector_today_tickers) if correlation_note: print(f" {correlation_note}") # Re-check the cap after sizing adjustment, in case this trade alone would blow through it if current_exposure + trade["total_risk"] > MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE: remaining = MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE - current_exposure if remaining < 50: print(f" SKIP — {sector} exposure cap reached, no room remaining") continue scale = remaining / trade["total_risk"] trade["contracts"] = max(1, int(trade["contracts"] * scale)) trade["total_risk"] = round(trade["total_risk"] * scale, 2) trade["position_pct"] = round(trade["position_pct"] * scale, 1) correlation_note = (correlation_note or "") + f" | Further reduced to fit remaining ${remaining:.0f} sector cap" log_trade(trade) headlines = get_news_headlines(ticker) send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime, correlation_note=correlation_note) mark_alerted(ticker) sector_alert_count[sector] = sector_alert_count.get(sector, 0) + 1 new_alerts += 1 ``` Now update `send_alert_email()` to accept and display the correlation note. Find: ```python def send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime): ``` Replace with: ```python def send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime, correlation_note=None): ``` Then find this part of the HTML body (right after the "Trade Setup" div): ```python

``` Add this right before it: ```python {f'''

⚠️ Correlation Notice

{correlation_note}

''' if correlation_note else ''}

``` Also remove the now-redundant `MAX_ALERTS_PER_SECTOR` constraint we added earlier — find: ```python MAX_ALERTS_PER_SECTOR = 1 ``` Replace with: ```python # Replaced by correlation-aware sizing + MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE cap below ``` And find/remove the old hard cap check inside the loop: ```python if sector_alert_count.get(sector, 0) >= MAX_ALERTS_PER_SECTOR: continue ``` Delete that block entirely — the new dollar-based exposure check replaces it. Save with **Command + S**, verify, and test: ``` python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/live_monitor.py'); print('No syntax errors!')" python3 agents/live_monitor.py ``` This version will now: let an entire genuinely-moving sector get fully surfaced to you, automatically shrink position size for each additional correlated name in that sector, and hard-stop once total dollar risk in that sector for the day hits 15% of your account — giving you the visibility you wanted without the unbounded correlated risk that caused today's confusion. current_exposure if remaining < 50: print(f" SKIP — {sector} exposure cap reached, no room remaining") continue scale = remaining / trade["total_risk"] trade["contracts"] = max(1, int(trade["contracts"] * scale)) trade["total_risk"] = round(trade["total_risk"] * scale, 2) trade["position_pct"] = round(trade["position_pct"] * scale, 1) extra_note = f"Further reduced to fit remaining ${remaining:.0f} sector cap" correlation_note = f"{correlation_note} | {extra_note}" if correlation_note else extra_note log_trade(trade) headlines = get_news_headlines(ticker) send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime, correlation_note=correlation_note) mark_alerted(ticker) new_alerts += 1 time.sleep(1.5) print(f"\n{'='*50}") print(f"LIVE MONITOR COMPLETE") print(f"Stocks checked: {checked}") print(f"New alerts sent: {new_alerts}") print(f"{'='*50}") if __name__ == "__main__": run_live_monitor() ``` Save with **Command + S**, verify, and run: ``` python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/live_monitor.py'); print('No syntax errors!')" python3 agents/live_monitor.py ``` Tell me what it prints — since your Daily Alerts Tracker likely already has today's date with no entries yet (or carries over from a prior test), this should run clean and either find nothing (if no new signal exists right now) or, if it does find something, show you the new correlation-aware sizing logic in action via the printed `SKIP` or correlation note lines.

macd_sig = float(signal_line.iloc[-1]) vol_now = float(df["Volume"].iloc[-1]) vol_avg_val = float(vol_avg.iloc[-1]) if any(v != v for v in [price, rsi, macd_val, macd_sig, bb_low, ma50_val, ma200_val]): return None volume_spike = vol_now > vol_avg_val lookback = min(20, len(closes) - 1) stock_return = (closes.iloc[-1] / closes.iloc[-lookback] - 1) * 100 rel_strength = float(stock_return) - float(etf_return) if rel_strength != rel_strength: rel_strength = 0 mr_score = 0 if rsi < 35: mr_score += 0.5 if price <= bb_low: mr_score += 0.5 if macd_val > macd_sig: mr_score += 0.5 trend_score = 0 if ma50_val > ma200_val: trend_score += 1.0 if price > ma50_val: trend_score += 1.0 sector_score = 0 if rel_strength > 0.02: sector_score = 1.5 elif rel_strength > 0: sector_score = 0.75 vol_score = 0.5 if volume_spike else 0 if rsi < 40 and price <= bb_low: trade_mode = "MEAN_REVERSION" weighted = (mr_score * 1.5) + (trend_score * 0.5) + sector_score + vol_score elif ma50_val > ma200_val and price > ma50_val: trade_mode = "MOMENTUM" weighted = (mr_score * 0.5) + (trend_score * 1.5) + sector_score + vol_score else: trade_mode = "NEUTRAL" weighted = mr_score + trend_score + sector_score + vol_score score = round(min(weighted, 10), 2) return { "ticker": ticker, "sector": sector, "price": price, "score": score, "trend_score": trend_score, "sector_score": sector_score, "mr_score": mr_score, "vol_score": vol_score, "trade_mode": trade_mode, "rsi": rsi } except Exception: return None def get_news_headlines(ticker, max_items=4): try: stock = yf.Ticker(ticker) news = spread.get("short_iv", 0) else: spread_desc = f"Buy ${spread['long_strike']}C / Sell ${spread['short_strike']}C" credit_text = f"Debit: ${spread['net_debit']}" iv = spread.get("long_iv", 0) news_html LOW and AVOID are skipped entirely -- the # deduplicated backtest showed those barely beat a coin flip. if rule["confidence"] not in ("HIGH", "MEDIUM"): continue if rule["confidence"] == "HIGH" and sig["score"] < 4.0: continue if rule["confidence"] == "MEDIUM" and sig["score"] < 4.5: continue try: ml_prob = predict_win_probability( score=sig["score"], trend_score=sig["trend_score"], sector_score=sig["sector_score"], mr_score=sig["mr_score"], vol_score=sig["vol_score"], vix=vix_price, sector=sector, mode=sig["trade_mode"], regime=regime ) except Exception: ml_prob = 50.0 if ml_prob < ML_PROBABILITY_FLOOR: continue print(f"\n>>> SIGNAL FOUND: {ticker} | Score {sig['score']} | ML {ml_prob}% | {sig['trade_mode']} + {regime} | {rule['confidence']}") trade = analyze_trade(ticker, sector, sig["score"], sig["trade_mode"], regime, sig["price"], STARTING_CAPITAL) if trade is None: print(f" Could not build options trade for {ticker}, skipping alert") continue # Check existing sector exposure today -- hard dollar cap regardless # of correlation, prevents one sector from dominating the whole day sector_today_tickers = get_todays_alerted_tickers_with_sector(sector) current_exposure = get_sector_dollar_exposure(sector_today_tickers) if current_exposure >= MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE: print(f" SKIP — {sector} already at ${current_exposure:.0f} exposure today (cap: ${MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE:.0f})") continue # Scale down size if correlated with something already alerted today trade, correlation_note = adjust_size_for_correlation(trade, sector_today_tickers) if correlation_note: print(f" {correlation_note}") # Re-check the cap after sizing adjustment if current_exposure + trade["total_risk"] > MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE: remaining = MAX_SECTOR_DOLLAR_EXPOSURE - current_exposure if remaining < 50: print(f" SKIP — {sector} exposure cap reached, no room remaining") continue scale = remaining / trade["total_risk"] trade["contracts"] = max(1, int(trade["contracts"] * scale)) trade["total_risk"] = round(trade["total_risk"] * scale, 2) trade["position_pct"] = round(trade["position_pct"] * scale, 1) extra_note = f"Further reduced to fit remaining ${remaining:.0f} sector cap" correlation_note = f"{correlation_note} | {extra_note}" if correlation_note else extra_note log_trade(trade) headlines = get_news_headlines(ticker) send_alert_email(trade, ml_prob, headlines, regime, correlation_note=correlation_note) mark_alerted(ticker) new_alerts += 1 time.sleep(1.5) print(f"\n{'='*50}") print(f"LIVE MONITOR COMPLETE") print(f"Stocks checked: {checked}") print(f"New alerts sent: {new_alerts}") print(f"{'='*50}") if __name__ == "__main__": run_live_monitor() ``` Save with **Command + S**, verify, and run: ``` python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/live_monitor.py'); print('No syntax errors!')" python3 agents/live_monitor.py ``` Tell me what it prints — since your Daily Alerts Tracker likely already has today's date with no entries yet (or carries over from a prior test), this should run clean and either find nothing (if no new signal exists right now) or, if it does find something, show you the new correlation-aware sizing logic in action via the printed `SKIP` or correlation note lines.

You've actually already shown the instinct that *is* the next level — today wasn't really about adding features, it was about learning to distrust your own system's output until you'd verified it, and that's the rarest and most valuable skill in building something like this. Most people who build a trading bot never get past "look, it made money" — you got to "wait, why did it make money, let me check the math" on day one of having real paper trades. That's worth more than any single feature we could add tomorrow.

So let me reframe "next level" less as a feature list and more as a few honest tracks, because they pull in different directions and it's worth being deliberate about which one you actually want:

**Track 1 — Trustworthiness (where today's work lives).** Sanity-checkers, better validation, walk-forward testing, catching bugs before they fool you. This track doesn't make the bot "smarter" in a flashy way — it makes you able to actually believe the numbers it gives you. This is the unglamorous, compounding-value track. Most retail systems skip it entirely and that's exactly why most retail systems blow up.

**Track 2 — Genuine signal edge.** Better data (Polygon.io, real historical options chains, Quiver Quantitative for institutional flow), the news agent with real Claude-summarized sentiment, more sophisticated ML (feature engineering, regime-specific models, walk-forward validation). This is "find more truth in the data."

**Track 3 — Operational maturity.** What you did today with the GitHub billing scare is actually this track — making the system survive contact with reality (rate limits, costs, sleep/crash failures, dead tickers). A system that's brilliant on paper but silently dies for a week because of an API limit isn't actually a system yet.

**Track 4 — Capital readiness.** Eventually: real brokerage API connection, actual order execution, the wheel strategy at scale, position sizing that's been proven not just modeled. This is the furthest out and the least urgent right now, since the paper system hasn't run long enough to trust yet.

**My honest read on what "next level" should mean for you specifically right now:** you're maybe 1 real bug discovery away from actually being able to trust your closed-trade history, and zero real weeks into seeing how the corrected system performs over time. The single highest-leverage thing isn't a new feature — it's **time and repetition**. Let the corrected system run untouched for 2-3 weeks, let positions actually close for real reasons (profit target, stop loss, or expiration), and then look at *that* data instead of backtested or just-opened data. Right now you literally cannot know if today's fix made things better, because nothing has closed yet under the corrected math.

If you want a concrete "next level" feeling sooner than that, the sanity-checker (tomorrow's Step 2) is genuinely the right next move — it's the thing that lets you trust results faster going forward, which is the actual bottleneck, not signal sophistication. Building a smarter model on top of unverified data just means you can be fooled in a more sophisticated way.

Does that framing help, or is there a specific track among those four that's pulling at you more than the others?

Good question to revisit now that you have a much cleaner foundation than when we first discussed it — the corrected options engine and the deduplicated backtest mean what you'd port over is actually trustworthy this time, not built on the inflated numbers from earlier today.

Here's the honest implementation path, broken into what's actually involved at each stage:

**What QuantConnect actually gives you that you don't have:**

Real historical options chains — actual bid/ask, actual IV, actual greeks, for every expiration, every day, going back years. Your current options backtest doesn't exist yet in the strict sense — you've only ever priced options using the *live* chain (today's prices) when testing trades in real time via `options_strategy.py`. The stock-price-based backtest (`data/backtest.py`) never touched real options pricing at all; it inferred outcomes purely from underlying stock returns. QuantConnect closes that gap completely.

**Step 1 — Account setup and environment.**

Sign up free at quantconnect.com. They give you a cloud-based Python IDE (their "Lean" engine) plus free access to US equity options history for backtesting — no separate Polygon/CBOE subscription needed for this. You write strategies in Python using their `QCAlgorithm` base class, which is different syntax from raw `yfinance`/`pandas` but conceptually similar (you're still computing indicators and making decisions on bars of data).

**Step 2 — Port the signal logic, not the infrastructure.**

You will NOT port `outcomes.py`, `agents/sentiment.py`, Google Sheets logging, or email reporting — none of that exists in QuantConnect's world and none of it needs to. What you port is purely the **decision logic**:
- The RSI/MACD/Bollinger/trend/sector-relative-strength math from `calculate_signals()` in `data/backtest.py`
- The mode classification (MEAN_REVERSION / MOMENTUM / NEUTRAL)
- The regime classification logic from `agents/sentiment.py` (VIX, SPY vs MAs, breadth, etc.)
- The `STRATEGY_RULES` confidence-tier mapping you've already corrected today

Let's build the sanity-checker. The goal: after every `check_open_trades()` run, automatically scan the results for patterns that *should* make you suspicious — the kind of thing you caught by eye yesterday — and flag them clearly instead of relying on you noticing. **What it should catch, based on yesterday's actual bug:** 1. **Suspiciously fast closes** — any trade that closes within 1-2 days of opening (real options spreads rarely hit 50% profit that fast unless something unusual happened) 2. **Suspiciously uniform outcomes** — multiple trades closing at nearly the same time with nearly the same PnL%, which is the fingerprint of a systematic bug rather than independent market moves 3. **100% win streaks** — any closed-trade win rate of 100% across more than ~5 trades, which is statistically implausible given your own backtest showing 53-65% win rates even in your best combinations 4. **PnL math inconsistency** — checking that reported PnL is actually mathematically possible given the logged credit/debit and max_loss at entry (catches a future version of yesterday's bug directly) 5. **Correlation clustering blindness** — flags if many "independent" wins are actually the same sector moving together, so you don't mistake one lucky sector rally for broad system validation In VS Code create a new file `agents/sanity_checker.py` and paste this: ```python import gspread import sys import os from datetime import datetime, timedelta from collections import defaultdict sys.path.append(os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))) from google.oauth2.service_account import Credentials from config import NOTIFICATION_EMAIL, GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD import smtplib from email.mime.text import MIMEText from email.mime.multipart import MIMEMultipart def get_sheets(): scope = ["https://spreadsheets.google.com/feeds", "https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive"] creds = Credentials.from_service_account_file("credentials.json", scopes=scope) client = gspread.authorize(creds) return client.open("Trading Bot Log") def load_closed_trades(): sheet = get_sheets() opt = sheet.worksheet("Options Paper Trades") rows = opt.get_all_values() trades = [] for row in rows[1:]: if len(row) < 24 or row[19] != "CLOSED": continue try: entry_date = datetime.strptime(row[1][:10], "%Y-%m-%d") exit_date = datetime.strptime(row[20][:10], "%Y-%m-%d") if row[20] else None trades.append({ "trade_id": row[0], "ticker": row[2], "sector": row[3], "strategy": row[4], "entry_date": entry_date, "exit_date": exit_date, "credit_debit": float(row[12]) if row[12] else 0, "max_loss": float(row[13]) if row[13] else 0, "total_risk": float(row[14]) if row[14] else 0, "contracts": int(row[11]) if row[11] else 1, "pnl": float(row[22]) if row[22] else 0, "pnl_pct": float(row[23]) if row[23] else 0, }) except Exception: continue return trades def check_fast_closes(trades, max_days=2): """Flag trades that closed suspiciously fast for an options spread.""" flagged = [] for t in trades: if t["exit_date"] is None: continue days_held = (t["exit_date"] - t["entry_date"]).days if 0 <= days_held <= max_days: flagged.append((t, days_held)) return flagged def check_win_streak(trades, min_sample=5): """Flag if win rate is suspiciously perfect across enough trades.""" if len(trades) < min_sample: return None wins = sum(1 for t in trades if t["pnl"] > 0) win_rate = wins / len(trades) * 100 if win_rate >= 95: return {"win_rate": round(win_rate, 1), "wins": wins, "total": len(trades)} return None def check_pnl_math(trades): """ Verify reported PnL is mathematically possible given credit/debit and max_loss logged at entry. Catches the exact class of bug from before -- PnL exceeding what the spread could ever actually pay out. """ flagged = [] for t in trades: max_possible_profit = abs(t["credit_debit"]) * t["contracts"] * 100 max_possible_loss = t["max_loss"] * t["contracts"] * 100 # Reported PnL should never exceed max possible profit (for a credit # spread) or be worse than max possible loss if t["pnl"] > max_possible_profit * 1.05: # 5% tolerance for rounding flagged.append((t, "PnL exceeds max possible profit", max_possible_profit)) elif t["pnl"] < -max_possible_loss * 1.05: flagged.append((t, "PnL exceeds max possible loss", -max_possible_loss)) return flagged def check_clustered_timing(trades, window_minutes=10): """ Flag if many trades opened or closed within the same tight window -- the fingerprint of one correlated sector event being mistaken for independent signals, not a bug per se, but worth surfacing. """ by_exit_window = defaultdict(list) for t in trades: if t["exit_date"] is None: continue key = t["exit_date"].strftime("%Y-%m-%d") by_exit_window[key].append(t) clusters = [] for date, group in by_exit_window.items(): if len(group) >= 5: sectors = set(t["sector"] for t in group) clusters.append({ "date": date, "count": len(group), "sectors_involved": len(sectors), "tickers": [t["ticker"] for t in group] }) return clusters def check_uniform_pnl_pct(trades, tolerance=3.0): """Flag if multiple trades closed with nearly identical PnL% -- another bug fingerprint, since real independent positions rarely converge this tightly.""" flagged_groups = [] sorted_trades = sorted(trades, key=lambda x: x["pnl_pct"]) i = 0 while i < len(sorted_trades): group = [sorted_trades[i]] j = i + 1 while j < len(sorted_trades) and abs(sorted_trades[j]["pnl_pct"] - sorted_trades[i]["pnl_pct"]) <= tolerance: group.append(sorted_trades[j]) j += 1 if len(group) >= 5: flagged_groups.append(group) i = j if j > i + 1 else i + 1 return flagged_groups def build_report(trades): issues = [] fast_closes = check_fast_closes(trades) if fast_closes: issues.append({ "severity": "HIGH", "title": f"{len(fast_closes)} trade(s) closed suspiciously fast (≤2 days)", "detail": [f"{t['ticker']} closed in {d} day(s), PnL ${t['pnl']:+.2f} ({t['pnl_pct']:+.1f}%)" for t, d in fast_closes[:10]] }) win_streak = check_win_streak(trades) if win_streak: issues.append({ "severity": "HIGH", "title": f"Suspiciously high win rate: {win_streak['win_rate']}% ({win_streak['wins']}/{win_streak['total']})", "detail": ["Your backtest showed 53-65% win rates even in your best combinations. A win rate this high across this many trades is not statistically plausible and likely indicates a pricing/valuation bug."] }) pnl_math_issues = check_pnl_math(trades) if pnl_math_issues: issues.append({ "severity": "CRITICAL", "title": f"{len(pnl_math_issues)} trade(s) with IMPOSSIBLE PnL math", "detail": [f"{t['ticker']}: {reason} (max possible: ${max_val:+.2f}, reported: ${t['pnl']:+.2f})" for t, reason, max_val in pnl_math_issues[:10]] }) clusters = check_clustered_timing(trades) if clusters: for c in clusters: severity = "MEDIUM" if c["sectors_involved"] <= 2 else "LOW" issues.append({ "severity": severity, "title": f"{c['count']} trades closed same day ({c['date']}) across only {c['sectors_involved']} sector(s)", "detail": [f"Tickers: {', '.join(c['tickers'])}", "This is likely ONE correlated sector move counted as multiple independent results."] }) uniform_groups = check_uniform_pnl_pct(trades) if uniform_groups: for g in uniform_groups: issues.append({ "severity": "MEDIUM", "title": f"{len(g)} trades closed with nearly identical PnL% (within 3%)", "detail": [f"{t['ticker']}: {t['pnl_pct']:+.1f}%" for t in g[:10]] }) return issues def send_sanity_report(issues, total_trades_checked): severity_color = {"CRITICAL": "#e74c3c", "HIGH": "#f39c12", "MEDIUM": "#f1c40f", "LOW": "#888"} severity_order = {"CRITICAL": 0, "HIGH": 1, "MEDIUM": 2, "LOW": 3} issues_sorted = sorted(issues, key=lambda x: severity_order.get(x["severity"], 9)) now = datetime.now().strftime("%B %d, %Y %I:%M %p CDT") if not issues: subject = f"✅ Sanity Check Passed — {total_trades_checked} closed trades reviewed" body_intro = f'

No suspicious patterns found across {total_trades_checked} closed trades. Results look statistically plausible.

' else: critical_count = sum(1 for i in issues if i["severity"] == "CRITICAL") subject = f"{'🚨' if critical_count else '⚠️'} Sanity Check: {len(issues)} issue(s) flagged" body_intro = f'

{len(issues)} issue(s) found across {total_trades_checked} closed trades reviewed. Review before trusting these results.

' issues_html = "" for issue in issues_sorted: color = severity_color.get(issue["severity"], "#888") detail_html = "".join(f'
  • {d}
    • ' for d in issue["detail"]) issues_html += f"""

    {issue['severity']} — {issue['title']}

      {detail_html}
    """ html = f"""

    🔍 Sanity Check Report

    {now}

    {body_intro} {issues_html}

    Trading Bot — Automated Sanity Check

    """ try: msg = MIMEMultipart("alternative") msg['From'] = NOTIFICATION_EMAIL msg['To'] = NOTIFICATION_EMAIL msg['Subject'] = subject msg.attach(MIMEText(html, 'html')) server = smtplib.SMTP('smtp.gmail.com', 587) server.starttls() server.login(NOTIFICATION_EMAIL, GMAIL_APP_PASSWORD) server.send_message(msg) server.quit() print(f"Sanity check report sent: {subject}") except Exception as e: print(f"Error sending sanity report: {e}") def run_sanity_check(send_email=True): print("=" * 50) print("RUNNING SANITY CHECK ON CLOSED TRADES") print("=" * 50) trades = load_closed_trades() print(f"Loaded {len(trades)} closed trades") if not trades: print("No closed trades to check yet.") return issues = build_report(trades) print(f"\nFound {len(issues)} issue(s):\n") for issue in issues: print(f"[{issue['severity']}] {issue['title']}") for d in issue["detail"]: print(f" {d}") print() if not issues: print("No suspicious patterns detected. Results look statistically plausible.") if send_email: send_sanity_report(issues, len(trades)) print("=" * 50) print("SANITY CHECK COMPLETE") print("=" * 50) if __name__ == "__main__": run_sanity_check() ``` Save with **Command + S**, verify, and run it against your current (now-corrected, all-open) trade history: ``` python3 -c "import py_compile; py_compile.compile('agents/sanity_checker.py'); print('No syntax errors!')" python3 agents/sanity_checker.py ``` Since all 22 positions are currently OPEN (none closed yet under the corrected math), this should report "No closed trades to check yet" — which is the expected, correct result right now. The real test comes once positions actually start closing for real reasons over the next few weeks; at that point this will run automatically and tell you immediately if anything looks statistically implausible, instead of you having to notice it yourself like yesterday. Want me to also wire this into `main.py` so it runs automatically after every `check_open_trades()` call (catching issues continuously) rather than only when you manually run it?

A win rate this high across this many trades is not statistically plausible and likely indicates a pricing/valuation bug."] }) pnl_math_issues = check_pnl_math(trades) if pnl_math_issues: issues.append({ "severity": "CRITICAL", "title": f"{len(pnl_math_issues)} trade(s) with IMPOSSIBLE PnL math", "detail": [f"{t['ticker']}: {reason} (max possible: ${max_val:+.2f}, reported: ${t['pnl']:+.2f})" for t, reason, max_val in pnl_math_issues[:10]] }) clusters = check_clustered_timing(trades) if clusters: for c in clusters: severity = "MEDIUM" if c["sectors_involved"] <= 2 else "LOW" issues.append({ "severity": severity, "title": f"{c['count']} trades closed same day ({c['date']}) across only {c['sectors_involved']} sector(s)", "detail": [f"Tickers: {', '.join(c['tickers'])}", "This is likely ONE correlated sector move counted as multiple independent results."] }) uniform_groups = check_uniform_pnl_pct(trades) if uniform_groups: for g in uniform_groups: issues.append({ "severity": "MEDIUM", "title": f"{len(g)} trades closed with nearly identical PnL% (within 3%)", "detail": [f"{t['ticker']}: {t['pnl_pct']:+.1f}%" for t in g[:10]] }) return issues def send_sanity_report(issues, total_trades_checked): severity_color = {"CRITICAL": "#e74c3c", "HIGH": "#f39c12", "MEDIUM": "#f1c40f", "LOW": "#888"} severity_order = {"CRITICAL": 0, "HIGH": 1, "MEDIUM": 2, "LOW": 3} issues_sorted = sorted(issues, key=lambda x: severity_order.get(x["severity"], 9)) now = datetime.now().strftime("%B %d, %Y %I:%M %p CDT") if not issues: subject = f"✅ Sanity Check Passed — {total_trades_checked} closed trades reviewed" body_intro = f'

No suspicious patterns found across {total_trades_checked} closed trades. Results look statistically plausible.

' else: critical_count = sum(1 for i in issues if i["severity"] == "CRITICAL") subject = f"{'🚨' if critical_count else '⚠️'} Sanity Check: {len(issues)} issue(s) flagged" body_intro = f'

{len(issues)} issue(s) found across {total_trades_checked} closed trades reviewed. Review before trusting these results.

  • {d}

def get_last_check_time():
    try:
        sheet = get_sheets()
        try:
            tracker = sheet.worksheet("Sanity Check Log")
        except gspread.exceptions.WorksheetNotFound:
            tracker = sheet.add_worksheet(title="Sanity Check Log", rows=500, cols=4)
            tracker.append_row(["Timestamp", "Trades Checked", "Issues Found", "Emailed"])
            return None
        rows = tracker.get_all_values()
        if len(rows) < 2:
            return None
        last_ts = rows[-1][0]
        return datetime.strptime(last_ts, "%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
    except Exception:
        return None


def log_check_run(trades_checked, issues_found, emailed):
    try:
        sheet = get_sheets()
        tracker = sheet.worksheet("Sanity Check Log")
        now = datetime.now().strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M")
        tracker.append_row([now, trades_checked, issues_found, str(emailed)])
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Error logging sanity check run: {e}")


def run_sanity_check(send_email=True, force_email=False):
    """
    Always runs the checks and prints to console. Email logic:
      - Never emails if there are zero closed trades yet (nothing to check)
      - Always emails immediately if any issue is found, regardless of severity
      - If no issues found, only emails once per ~24 hours (a quiet daily
        "all clear" rather than spamming every single scheduled run)
      - force_email=True overrides the 24h throttle (used for manual runs)
    """
    print("=" * 50)
    print("RUNNING SANITY CHECK ON CLOSED TRADES")
    print("=" * 50)

    trades = load_closed_trades()
    print(f"Loaded {len(trades)} closed trades")

    if not trades:
        print("No closed trades to check yet -- nothing to validate.")
        print("=" * 50)
        return

    issues = build_report(trades)

    print(f"\nFound {len(issues)} issue(s):\n")
    for issue in issues:
        print(f"[{issue['severity']}] {issue['title']}")
        for d in issue["detail"]:
            print(f"    {d}")
        print()

    if not issues:
        print("No suspicious patterns detected. Results look statistically plausible.")

    should_email = False
    if send_email:
        if issues:
            should_email = True  # always alert immediately on any flagged issue
        elif force_email:
            should_email = True
        else:
            last_check = get_last_check_time()
            if last_check is None or (datetime.now() - last_check) > timedelta(hours=24):
                should_email = True

    if should_email:
        send_sanity_report(issues, len(trades))
        log_check_run(len(trades), len(issues), True)
    else:
        print("Skipping email (no issues found, checked recently).")
        log_check_run(len(trades), len(issues), False)

    print("=" * 50)
    print("SANITY CHECK COMPLETE")
    print("=" * 50)
```
